nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone of Lake Malawi; found among sediment-free rocky shores where aufwuchs (algae and associated microorganisms) is grazed from rock surfaces

decorPreferences: extensive rockwork with caves and crevices
stacked rocks forming territories and hiding spots
open sandy substrate (no gravel sharp enough to injure)
minimal or no planting (plants typically uprooted or eaten)
rockwork piles reaching near the surface

diet.notes: Primarily an aufwuchs grazer in the wild — scrapes algae and the microorganisms (rotifers, small crustaceans, insect larvae) embedded within it from rock surfaces. In captivity, feed spirulina-based flakes or pellets as the staple; supplement with blanched vegetables (spinach, zucchini) and occasional high-protein foods such as mysis shrimp or daphnia. Avoid beef heart and mammalian proteins, which can cause Malawi bloat. Do not overfeed protein-rich foods.

goodWith: other Lake Malawi mbuna of similar aggression level and dissimilar coloration (e.g., Pseudotropheus, Labeotropheus, Labidochromis caeruleus)
other robust Lake Malawi cichlids that are not closely related to Melanochromis
avoidWith: other Melanochromis species — hybridizes readily with M. interruptus, M. cyaneorhabdos, and others; never mix congeners
Melanochromis auratus (Auratus Cichlid, fw-fish-146) — nearly identical coloration in females/juveniles leads to hybridization and severe intrageneric aggression
small tetras, rasboras, or other non-mbuna community fish
conspecific males unless tank is very large with broken lines of sight

imageCandidates.notes: Male in characteristic black-and-blue striped coloration. FishBase species page image.
Male specimen showing adult coloration. Wikimedia Commons contributor image.

summary: A pugnacious Lake Malawi mbuna with striking sexual dichromatism — mature males are black with vivid blue horizontal stripes, while females and juveniles are yellow-orange. A maternal mouthbrooder; intermediate care due to high aggression and strict water requirements.
careNotes: The Johanni Cichlid is a classic Lake Malawi mbuna from the rocky littoral zone and one of the more aggressive members of the genus Melanochromis. Maintain highly alkaline, hard water replicating Lake Malawi chemistry (pH 7.6–8.8, gH 10–25, kH 10–18); a crushed coral or aragonite substrate and limestone rockwork help buffer pH naturally. A minimum 200 L tank with extensive rockwork providing caves, crevices, and broken sightlines is essential — males establish and aggressively defend territories. Keep in a species-appropriate ratio of one male to three or more females to dilute male aggression; multiple males may be possible only in very large tanks (400 L+) with dense rockwork. Sexual dichromatism is pronounced: mature males develop black body coloration with two or three vivid blue horizontal stripes, while females and immature fish retain a yellow-orange coloration. Juveniles of both sexes are yellow, and males begin darkening at sexual maturity (approximately 4–6 months of age). This juvenile color phase means young males may be sold or misidentified as females. The yellow female coloration is superficially similar to that of the related Auratus Cichlid (Melanochromis auratus, fw-fish-146), and the two species must never be housed together — intergeneric hybridization and severe aggression are both documented. More broadly, never mix any Melanochromis species; M. johannii hybridizes readily with M. interruptus, M. cyaneorhabdos, and other congeners, corrupting both bloodlines. Overfeeding protein-rich foods is a leading cause of Malawi bloat (internal bacterial infection); spirulina-based staple diet with only occasional protein supplementation is strongly advised. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are recommended to maintain water quality.
breedingNotes: Melanochromis johannii is a maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning typically occurs on a flat rock surface; the female deposits eggs and immediately takes them into her buccal cavity after the male fertilizes them. She carries the eggs and developing fry for approximately 21–28 days, during which time she does not eat. Brood sizes typically range from 10 to 30 fry. Remove the brooding female to a separate tank or provide dense rockwork cover to protect her from male harassment. Fry are released as fully-formed miniatures and immediately capable of feeding on crushed spirulina flake and baby brine shrimp nauplii. The male's role ends at fertilization; no paternal care is provided. Breeding is generally straightforward once a compatible group is established, but the aggressive nature of males can stress or kill females if ratios are imbalanced.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Melanochromis johannii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Lake Malawi rocky littoral zone), water parameters (pH 7.5–8.5, temperature 24–28°C, hardness 179–447 ppm / ~10–25 dH), adult size (males to 12 cm, females slightly smaller), diet (aufwuchs grazer), mouthbrooding details, and husbandry guidance including Malawi bloat risk and congener hybridization warning.

additional.name: FishBase — Melanochromis johannii
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes), adult size (to 9.5 cm SL / ~12 cm TL), native range (Lake Malawi, Mozambique/Malawi), depth range (3–25 m), temperature range (23–27°C), diet classification (herbivore/aufwuchs grazer), and IUCN status (Least Concern). Also confirmed maternal mouthbrooder reproductive mode.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Mbuna Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed trade care guidance: tank size recommendation (55+ gal / ~200 L minimum for mbuna), rockwork requirements, alkaline hard water chemistry, diet (spirulina-heavy), aggression management (high female ratio), and mouthbrooder breeding overview applicable to M. johannii as a mbuna representative. Cross-referenced Johanni-specific coloration data.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Lake Malawi Mbuna species guide
additional.notes: Confirmed Melanochromis genus hybridization risk (never mix species), juvenile color phase (all-yellow juveniles, males darken at maturity), female/juvenile similarity between M. johannii and M. auratus, and dietary caution against mammalian proteins (Malawi bloat risk). Supported minimum group size of 6+ for behavioral normalization.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Melanochromis johannii species profile
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid community source. Confirmed adult male coloration (black body with blue horizontal stripes), female/juvenile yellow-orange coloration, hybrid risk with M. interruptus and M. cyaneorhabdos specifically named, mouthbrooding incubation period (~21–28 days), brood size (10–30 fry), and lifespan (5–8 years in captivity).

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Female carries eggs and fry buccally for 21–28 days; brood size 10–30. Remove brooding female to avoid harassment. Sexing straightforward in adults (male = black/blue; female = yellow), but juveniles are all yellow. No paternal care.
